comment.body:
as said, when you buy an option, you buy vol. 
you bet on or against the IV on it.
what is vol? the SD of the logreturns.
what does it mean? how much SD is expected for the price action. 
as I showed in the post about vol, this assumption is based on gaussian distribution, or normal distribution (same concept).
it is symmetrical, every sigma values have a probability values.

we can take the IV of a contract, multiply it by 1/sqrt(252) and so it reveals us the 1 sigma range that the market bets on for that contract alone. 
the common practice is to take the ATM IV on lets say weekly expo.
some use the ruole of 16, bcs sqrt(252) is close to 16... "good enough"
my calculation is a bit more complex, but the idea is to take the ranges that the market bets on

comment.body:
Have you guys read my post about market theories? there i cover mean reversion in short.
think of a simple normal distribution.
the lvls give you the ranges with probabilities assuming normal distribution, that the price will move in the range, means, rejecting the line. 
IV is the volatility that the market bets on. It is the risk premium in the option price. I take it and project the distribution that the priced IV covers.

So the mean reversion lvls give you idea about the price extremes where are the lvls where the participants expect reversion. 
You can use Kelly to size the probability according to the lvl values.

Risk lvl is momentum confirmer.

comment.body: You have to also consider your account size. An /MES futures contract is about 50X leverage that of a single SPY share. Holding a /MES contract more than 23 hours would require about $1,600 in margin (unaccounted account balance). That would be the equivalent of holding about 2.66666666 SPY shares overnight. So for the same margin (cash) requirements, an /MES contract is about 18 times the leverage of SPY dollar for dollar. So if you had, say, $4,800 per contract of cash, then you'd cut down the leverage by 3X, and you'd have about 6X the leverage of SPY for that amount of money.

comment.body: Now, as you mentioned, the nice thing about futures is no decay (but there can be if you are holding it for an extended period of time), and if you are in the US, futures are taxed at 60% long term capital gains and 40% short term. Options are 100% taxed as short term, so better for short term trades from a tax perpective. Now, if you had a large enough account balance to hold a longer term swing trade, there may be a chance for arbitrage (or decay) depending on where you believe the direction of the market is going and whether it is in contango or backwardation.

comment.body:
yes, it is like you are in a football cup and a bookmaker comes to you saying: "hey, wanna bet on the likelyhood that the likelyhood of the bet of XY on Les Bleus will go up?" 
would you bet on that? i guess you don't. It's sounds silly and fraudish.... but whenever somebody trades a VIX call, this is what he does. 
betting on something that is made up, that doesn't even exists.
But the wild thing is that across the forced hedging flows, this thing becomes self-fulfilling. this is why it is said that 'tail wagging the dog'.
think about that
